title: Cat Crying in the Litter Box: Is It an Emergency?
meta_desc: If your cat is crying while using the litter box, it may be a medical emergency. Learn the signs of urinary blockages and when to see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Crying in the litter box is a high-priority medical emergency that often indicates a life-threatening urinary blockage. Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if your cat is straining or unable to urinate.

What does it mean if my cat is crying while using the litter box?

When a cat cries or vocalizes loudly while inside the litter box, it is a clear sign of significant pain and distress. This behavior usually indicates that the cat is struggling to urinate or defecate. In many cases, it is associated with Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD), which can include bladder stones, severe infections, or a life-threatening urethral obstruction.

Is it a medical emergency if my cat is crying in the litter box?

  • Urgency Level: High.
  • If your cat is straining, crying, and producing little to no urine, you must treat this as a life-threatening emergency.
  • A urinary blockage is fatal if not treated quickly, as it causes toxins to build up in the bloodstream and can lead to heart failure within 24 to 48 hours.
  • Please contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How can taking a photo of the litter box help the veterinary triage team?

  • Taking a clear photo of the litter box contents can provide vital information for the veterinary triage team.
  • A photo allows the vet to see the presence of blood, the size and frequency of urine clumps, or the consistency of stool.
  • These visual cues help the medical team determine the severity of the blockage or inflammation before you even arrive at the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

If a cat is crying in the litter box, medical problems should be excluded or treated first. If the problem persists, focus on the behavioral history, including aspects of the substrate, box, or location that may reduce appeal; unpleasant experiences at or near the box (e.g., insufficient cleaning, noises, pain due to medical problems); or difficulty in gaining access to the box. Important historical details include whether the elimination is urine or feces, whether urine deposits are vertical (spraying) or horizontal (soiling), duration and frequency of the problem, signalment and temperament of the soiling cat, when and where the cat eliminates, litter box details (number, placement, cleaning, substrate, size), the cat's daily routine, and its home environment. Treatment should focus on providing a litter, box, and site that is most appealing to the cat; reducing or preventing the use of the soiled location; and resolving the underlying factors contributing to the soiling.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1573)

faqs.question: Can this happen to female cats?
faqs.answer: Yes. While urinary blockages are more common in male cats due to their anatomy, female cats can still experience painful urinary tract infections or bladder stones that cause them to cry out in pain.

faqs.question: My cat is still eating, is it still an emergency?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cats are experts at hiding illness. Vocalizing in the litter box is a sign of acute pain that overrides their natural instinct to hide discomfort, regardless of their appetite.

faqs.question: What if they are just constipated?
faqs.answer: Straining to defecate can look very similar to straining to urinate. Both conditions are painful and require veterinary intervention to prevent further complications like a megacolon or systemic toxicity.
